does: This paper investigates how the design of agent harnesses—the execution frameworks that deploy language models as autonomous agents—affects overall system performance. The authors identify and formalize the 'intent-execution gap': the mismatch between what a model intends to do and what the harness actually executes. They develop Simple Strands Agent (SSA), a customizable harness designed to minimize this gap by identifying common patterns that work across different model families (Claude, Gemini, GPT, Grok, Qwen) while incorporating model-specific preferences. The work demonstrates that careful harness design is as critical as model capabilities themselves, reproducing or improving pass@1 performance on popular agentic benchmarks like SWE-Pro through better alignment between model outputs and execution logic.
connects: This work builds on the growing recognition that deploying large language models as agents requires more than just scaling model size or capability. It extends prior research on agent architectures and tool use by shifting focus from what models can theoretically do toward what they actually accomplish in practice through harness interfaces. The paper responds to observations from recent work on model-based agents (like ReAct, Chain-of-Thought prompting, and tool-use frameworks) that showed systematic failures despite capable underlying models. It draws connections to research on prompt engineering, in-context learning, and the behavioral analysis of language models, while introducing a systems perspective that treats agent performance as a co-design problem between model and execution environment rather than solely a modeling problem.
enables: This framework enables future work on agent system design by providing a concrete lens for diagnosing performance bottlenecks. By decomposing agent failure into intent versus execution failures, the approach allows researchers to identify whether improvements should target model training, prompt engineering, or harness redesign. The emphasis on cross-model patterns and model-specific preferences creates a foundation for building more modular and transferable agent systems. The work opens directions for systematic study of how different harness designs affect model behavior across diverse tasks and model families, and suggests that optimizing this intent-execution alignment could unlock significant performance gains—potentially enabling more reliable and capable autonomous agents without requiring new model training.
